cards.back: A topological space is said to be locally connected at a point x if for every open set U containing x, there exists a connected open set V containing x such that U ⊆ V.

A topological space is said to be arc-wise connected if for every two points x and y in the space, there exists a continuous function f from the unit interval [0,1] to the space such that f(0) = x and f(1) = y.
A topological space is said to be locally arc-wise connected at a point x if for every neighborhood U of x, there exists a connected open neighborhood V of x such that V is arc-wise connected.

A space is said to satisfy the separation axiom T3 (also known as regularity) if it is Hausdorff and for every point x and every closed set A not containing x, there exists a continuous function f from the space to the interval [0,1] such that f(x) = 0 and f(A) = 1.
In a space that is arc-wise connected, every point is a local connectedness point, but the converse is not necessarily true.
